question: You are the principal investigator of a research project funded by a private technology company. During the final phase of the study, the experimental results reveal serious limitations in the company's product. When the sponsor reviews the preliminary report, they express concern that publishing such results may harm their market reputation. A representative informally suggests that your team reconsider the interpretation of the findings so the conclusions appear less damaging. At the same time, several junior researchers in your team are worried that refusing the sponsor's suggestion could result in loss of funding for future projects and affect their careers.  What would you do?
option1: Modify the interpretation slightly to maintain the collaboration
option2: Publish only favorable results and omit the negative findings
option3: Maintain complete research integrity and publish all findings transparently
option4: Delay publication indefinitely to avoid conflict

question: You are working with a multidisciplinary research group on a journal paper. A senior colleague from another department insists that their name be included as a co-author even though they contributed only minor suggestions during early discussions. Some members of your team believe refusing may create departmental tension and affect future collaborations, while others feel that including the colleague violates accepted authorship guidelines and academic integrity standards.
option1: Include the colleague as an author to avoid conflict
option2: Remove their name without discussion
option3: Initiate a professional discussion using established authorship guidelines to determine contributions fairly
option4: Withdraw the paper submission entirely

question: A research assistant working under your supervision submits a draft report for publication. While reviewing the document, you notice that several sections appear to be copied from previously published articles without proper citation. The assistant claims they were unaware of the seriousness of plagiarism and asks you not to report the issue because they fear losing their research position.
option1: Ignore the issue to avoid damaging the assistant’s career
option2: Remove the assistant from the project immediately
option3: Explain the seriousness of plagiarism and require the report to be corrected with proper citations before submission
option4: Submit the report anyway because the research results are valid

question: During an academic conference, a colleague shares an unpublished research idea with you during an informal discussion. Later, you realize that the idea could lead to a high-impact publication if developed further. Since the concept was not formally documented or presented publicly, you could potentially work on it independently without acknowledgment.
option1: Develop and publish the idea independently
option2: Ignore the idea completely
option3: Discuss collaboration with the colleague and acknowledge their contribution
option4: Modify the idea slightly and publish it as your own

question: A postgraduate student who has consistently performed well in previous semesters begins missing classes and submitting assignments late. During a brief conversation after class, the student admits feeling emotionally overwhelmed due to family problems and expresses uncertainty about continuing the program. University policies allow only limited extensions for assignments, and granting special consideration may raise concerns about fairness among other students.
option1: Deny any flexibility and enforce academic rules strictly
option2: Ignore the emotional concerns and focus only on academic performance
option3: Speak privately with the student, show empathy, and guide them toward counseling services while exploring possible academic accommodations
option4: Suggest that the student withdraw from the program

question: One of your students informs you that they experience severe anxiety when speaking in front of large groups. The course assessment requires an oral presentation, and the student worries that the anxiety may affect their performance and grades. Other students have completed similar presentations successfully.
option1: Require the student to present in the same way as everyone else
option2: Ignore the concern and assign a lower grade if performance is poor
option3: Offer supportive alternatives such as smaller presentations or gradual exposure while encouraging confidence building
option4: Remove presentations entirely from the course

question: During a mid-semester examination, you notice a student becoming visibly distressed. The student begins breathing rapidly and appears unable to concentrate on the paper. Other students in the exam hall start noticing the situation, creating a tense atmosphere.
option1: Ignore the student and continue the examination
option2: Ask the student to leave the examination hall immediately
option3: Calm the student, allow a brief pause if permitted by policy, and guide them toward counseling services afterward
option4: Mark the student absent for failing to complete the exam

question: Late in the evening, you receive an email from a student saying they feel hopeless about their future and are unable to manage academic pressure. The student requests guidance but also asks you not to inform anyone else.
option1: Ignore the message since it is not an academic issue
option2: Reply briefly and remind the student about assignment deadlines
option3: Respond with empathy, encourage professional counseling, and involve appropriate student support services if necessary
option4: Advise the student to take a permanent break from studies

question: At the end of the semester, student evaluations reveal that while your lectures are intellectually rigorous, many students find them difficult to follow. Some comments mention that the pace is too fast and that complex theoretical concepts are not always explained with practical examples.
option1: Ignore the feedback because the subject is inherently difficult
option2: Reduce the course rigor significantly
option3: Reflect on the feedback and redesign lectures to improve clarity while maintaining academic depth
option4: Stop collecting student feedback in future courses

question: Your classroom includes students from different linguistic and cultural backgrounds. Several international students report difficulty understanding certain explanations and participating in discussions.
option1: Continue teaching exactly the same way
option2: Provide supplementary learning materials and encourage inclusive participation
option3: Ask struggling students to study independently
option4: Simplify the course excessively

question: Students increasingly use artificial intelligence tools to generate parts of their assignments. While these tools can help learning, excessive dependence may reduce originality and critical thinking.
option1: Ban AI tools completely
option2: Ignore the issue
option3: Create guidelines for ethical AI use and design assignments requiring critical analysis
option4: Penalize students immediately without investigation

question: A student proposes an unconventional project idea that may not guarantee success but offers significant potential for creative learning. Some faculty members recommend rejecting the idea and replacing it with a standard project topic.
option1: Reject the idea immediately
option2: Encourage experimentation while guiding the student carefully
option3: Replace it with a safer project
option4: Ignore the proposal

question: Your institution is preparing for an upcoming NAAC accreditation cycle. Faculty members are required to submit records of teaching innovations, mentoring activities, and research outputs for the past five years. Some colleagues suggest recreating missing documentation so the department can receive a higher accreditation score.
option1: Recreate documents based on memory
option2: Refuse to participate in documentation
option3: Submit only verifiable records and help establish transparent documentation practices
option4: Copy documents from another institution’s report

question: The Internal Quality Assurance Cell requires faculty members to upload course outcomes, lesson plans, and student performance data regularly on the institutional portal. Many colleagues complain that the process increases workload and interferes with research activities.
option1: Ignore the reporting requirements
option2: Submit incomplete reports just to satisfy the system
option3: Develop a systematic workflow to manage academic records efficiently
option4: Assign the work entirely to students

question: During NBA accreditation preparation, faculty must implement Outcome-Based Education and map Course Outcomes with Program Outcomes. Some colleagues treat the process as mere paperwork and copy mapping tables from other institutions.
option1: Ignore the mapping process
option2: Copy mapping data from other universities
option3: Implement genuine outcome mapping and explain its academic value
option4: Submit incomplete documentation

question: While compiling NAAC data, you notice that some departments have slightly exaggerated student placement statistics to improve the institution’s ranking.
option1: Ignore the issue
option2: Support the exaggerated data for institutional benefit
option3: Recommend correcting the data and maintaining transparency
option4: Remove your name from the report

question: You are simultaneously handling several responsibilities including teaching multiple courses, supervising graduate research, and preparing a grant proposal. The deadline for the proposal coincides with an important teaching period.
option1: Cancel some lectures
option2: Ignore the research proposal
option3: Manage time effectively to balance both responsibilities
option4: Delegate all teaching tasks to assistants

question: During a conference presentation, another researcher publicly questions the methodology of your research and challenges some of your assumptions in front of the audience.
option1: Respond defensively and dismiss the criticism
option2: Ignore the question
option3: Engage in a constructive academic discussion and explain your reasoning
option4: Criticize the researcher personally

question: Your department is discussing major curriculum changes to align with industry requirements. Faculty members have strong and conflicting opinions about the proposed changes, and meetings often become heated.
option1: Avoid involvement
option2: Support only your own interests
option3: Facilitate constructive dialogue and evidence-based decisions
option4: Publicly criticize colleagues

question: During accreditation preparation, several colleagues appear stressed due to long working hours and documentation pressure. Some faculty members become frustrated and productivity declines.
option1: Ignore colleagues’ stress and focus only on your work
option2: Criticize colleagues for complaining
option3: Promote teamwork, distribute tasks effectively, and encourage short breaks
option4: Delay all accreditation work
